meta.title: AI Security Controls Assessment — AICM Variant
meta.subtitle: Coalition for Secure AI · CoSAI AI SRF five layers, with AICM v1.0.3 control mapping and EU AI Act risk tiers
sources: CoSAI AI Shared Responsibility Framework, V0.7 (2026)

context.intro: Brief context helps calibrate which controls are most critical. All data stays in this browser — nothing is transmitted.

l1.name: AI Business & Usage
l1.title: L1 — AI Business & Usage
l1.meta: ~4 min · Governance, acceptable use, EU AI Act risk tiers
l1.intro: L1 covers how the deploying organization governs AI: risk appetite, acceptable-use policy, sector-specific regulation (FDA AI/ML, SR 11-7, EU AI Act), and accountability for AI System Users and AI System Governance personas. Per the SRF, L1 is end-user / organization owned across every operating model. AICM domain: GRC

l2.name: AI Information
l2.title: L2 — AI Information
l2.meta: ~4 min · Data governance, IAM, content filtering, shadow AI
l2.intro: L2 covers the information that flows through AI systems: data classification, training/RAG data governance, identity & access management, content filtering, agentic information flows, and shadow-AI usage. The Data Provider persona is accountable for upstream data quality, provenance, and licensing. AICM domains: DSP · CEK · STA · IAM

l3.name: AI Application
l3.title: L3 — AI Application
l3.meta: ~6 min · OWASP LLM Top 10 · Tool use · Agent autonomy (L0–L5)
l3.intro: L3 covers how AI capability is wrapped and exposed: application design, AI plugins / data connections, application safety systems, integration security, and (for agentic systems) tool & API access controls, telemetry, human override tiers (T1–T5), and autonomy classification (L0–L5). Application Developer is accountable; Agentic Platform Provider supports. AICM domains: AIS · MDS · LOG

l4.name: AI Platform
l4.title: L4 — AI Platform
l4.meta: ~4 min · Model serving, runtime isolation, IAM primitives, SLAs
l4.intro: L4 covers the platform services that host and serve models: model serving runtime, fine-tuning infrastructure, AI compute, IAM primitives, data residency, encryption, and platform-level attestations (SOC 2, ISO 27001, FedRAMP). Cloud / AI Platform Provider is accountable; AI Model Serving persona handles secure orchestration and delivery. AICM domains: I&S · IAM · LOG · TVM · A&A
l4.next: Continue to L5 — AI Model Provider →

l5.name: AI Model Provider
l5.title: L5 — AI Model Provider
l5.meta: ~4 min · Training data governance, model dev security, provenance
l5.intro: L5 covers the foundation-model supply chain: training data governance, bias detection, privacy-preserving training, dataset lineage, model adversarial testing, model cards, SBOM for model artifacts, cryptographic signing, and version / vulnerability disclosure. Model Provider (e.g. OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, IBM) is accountable. AICM domains: STA · DSP · GRC

l1.label: AI risk register reviewed quarterly
l1.why: Maintains a living inventory of AI-related risks enabling proactive mitigation before incidents occur. Without it, risks are discovered reactively after harm.

l1.label: Board / executive AI risk briefing conducted annually
l1.why: Executive oversight is a foundational control. Without board-level accountability, AI risk is under-resourced and incidents escalate without authority to act.

l1.label: AI use case approval process documented
l1.why: Undocumented approval processes lead to shadow AI and unreviewed deployments. A defined gate prevents high-risk use cases reaching production without review.

l1.label: Autonomy level (L0–L5) classified per AI system
l1.why: Autonomous agents require stronger controls than advisory tools. Without autonomy classification, human-in-the-loop thresholds and override mechanisms cannot be correctly calibrated.

l1.label: AI incident response plan documented and tested
l1.why: AI system failures differ from software failures — model drift, adversarial inputs, and output hallucinations require specialized playbooks separate from standard IT incident response.

l1.label: AI ethics and acceptable use policy established
l1.why: Defines what AI systems are permitted to do, preventing mission creep into high-risk use cases without appropriate controls or review.

l1.label: EU AI Act risk tier assigned per use case
l1.why: High-risk AI systems under the EU AI Act require conformity assessments, human oversight, and ongoing monitoring. Without per-use-case risk tier classification, compliance obligations go unrecognised and unresourced.

l2.label: Data classified for 100% of AI training assets
l2.why: Unclassified training data leads to uncontrolled access and GDPR/CCPA exposure. Classification is the prerequisite for all other data controls.

l2.label: Training data encrypted at rest (AES-256)
l2.why: Training datasets often contain sensitive information. Encryption at rest protects against storage-layer breaches and insider threat scenarios.

l2.label: Training data provenance and lineage documented
l2.why: Without provenance, poisoned or biased datasets cannot be traced or remediated. Required for NIST AI RMF MAP 2.3 and EU AI Act Art. 10 compliance.

l2.label: GDPR / CCPA data opt-out consent proof collected
l2.why: Using personal data in training without consent creates regulatory liability. Consent proof must be retained and verifiable at audit time.

l2.label: Prompt / inference data excluded from model training by default
l2.why: Production inference data often contains user PII. Without explicit training exclusion, this data may be incorporated in fine-tuning, creating disclosure risk.

l2.label: Access logging enabled on AI data stores (retain ≥ 90 days)
l2.why: Audit trails on training data access are required to detect insider threats, data poisoning attempts, and to support breach investigation.

l2.label: Data deletion certificates on file from AI vendors
l2.why: Vendors with access to training data must certify deletion upon contract termination. Without certificates, data may persist in third-party environments indefinitely.

l3.label: Prompt injection testing completed in CI/CD
l3.why: Prompt injection is the #1 LLM threat vector. Testing in CI/CD catches regressions before deployment. Manual-only testing is insufficient for fast-moving model updates.

l3.label: LLM output validated before execution or rendering
l3.why: Unsanitized LLM output routed to code execution, SQL, or HTML leads to injection vulnerabilities. Output must be treated as untrusted input from an external system.

l3.label: SAST / DAST integrated in AI application CI/CD pipeline
l3.why: Static and dynamic analysis catches code-level vulnerabilities introduced by AI-assisted development or conventional injection flaws in the application layer.

l3.label: Secrets scanning enabled on AI application repositories
l3.why: LLM applications commonly include API keys and model tokens in code or configuration. Secrets scanning prevents credential exposure in source control.

l3.label: Human override mechanisms implemented and tested (T1–T5)
l3.why: Agentic AI systems require graduated human control tiers. Without tested override mechanisms, autonomous systems cannot be safely halted during abnormal behavior.

l3.label: Agent actions and tool calls logged with full telemetry
l3.why: Agent trajectories must be auditable. Without telemetry, post-incident forensics is impossible and drift from intended behavior goes undetected.

l3.label: SBOM generated for AI model components and dependencies
l3.why: AI applications inherit supply chain risk from their model dependencies. An SBOM enables rapid identification of vulnerable or poisoned components.

l4.label: CIS Level 2 hardening baseline enforced on AI compute nodes
l4.why: AI compute environments are high-value targets. CIS L2 removes default credentials, unnecessary services, and misconfigurations that enable lateral movement.

l4.label: EDR deployed on all AI training and inference nodes
l4.why: Endpoint detection is required to identify cryptojacking, unauthorized model access, and exfiltration attempts in AI compute environments.

l4.label: Critical patch SLA met (critical vulnerabilities: ≤ 24 hours)
l4.why: Unpatched ML frameworks and AI libraries are a primary attack vector. Documented and measured SLAs are required to demonstrate control effectiveness.

l4.label: Inference environment isolated from training environment
l4.why: Shared inference/training environments create risk of training data contamination and model theft. Network and storage isolation is a critical boundary.

l4.label: MFA enforced for all AI platform administrators
l4.why: Admin access to AI infrastructure is a high-value target. MFA prevents credential-stuffing and phishing attacks from reaching control planes.

l4.label: AI workload events collected in centralised SIEM / logging
l4.why: AI systems produce distinctive telemetry (query volumes, latency spikes, unusual input patterns) that requires centralised collection to detect abuse.

l4.label: Tenant isolation architecture documented for multi-tenant AI services
l4.why: Multi-tenant AI services must prevent cross-tenant data leakage. Without documented isolation architecture, security reviews cannot verify the boundary.

l4.label: SOC 2 Type II report current (< 12 months) from AI platform provider
l4.why: Platform vendors must demonstrate operational security through independent audit. An expired or absent SOC 2 cannot be used to satisfy customer compliance obligations.

l5.label: Model cards received from all Cat2 vendors (model / foundation model providers)
l5.why: Model cards document training data, intended use, limitations, and known biases. Without them, risk assessment of the model is blind to its provenance.

l5.label: SOC 2 / ISO 27001 certification current for all Cat1–5 vendors
l5.why: Third-party vendors are a primary attack surface. Expired certifications indicate degraded security posture that may not be visible to customers.

l5.label: Sub-processor lists current and reviewed (< 6 months)
l5.why: AI vendors typically use sub-processors for compute, storage, and labeling. Outdated sub-processor lists prevent identification of fourth-party risks.

l5.label: SBOM generated for AI model artifacts and published to VRM
l5.why: Model SBOMs enable rapid response to compromised base models or dependencies. Without them, the blast radius of supply chain attacks cannot be assessed.

l5.label: Vendor Risk Assessment (VRA) questionnaire completed per vendor category
l5.why: Generic vendor questionnaires miss AI-specific risks. Category-specific VRA (Model Provider, Infrastructure, Data Labeling) surfaces controls relevant to the vendor's role.

l5.label: Cryptographic signing verified for all deployed model artifacts
l5.why: Unsigned models cannot be verified as unmodified from the source. Cryptographic signing detects tampering and supply chain substitution attacks.

l5.label: IP ownership and data destruction obligations documented (consulting / Cat4)
l5.why: Professional services engagements often have de facto access to models and proprietary data. Without documented obligations, post-engagement data retention is uncontrolled.

l5.label: EU AI Act risk category mapped per deployed model
l5.why: High-risk AI systems require conformity assessments and ongoing monitoring. Without per-model risk tier mapping, compliance obligations may be unrecognised.

owasp.name: Prompt Injection
owasp.desc: Manipulating LLM behavior via crafted inputs to bypass guardrails or exfiltrate data

owasp.name: Insecure Output Handling
owasp.desc: Unvalidated LLM output routed to code execution, SQL, or rendering contexts

owasp.name: Training Data Poisoning
owasp.desc: Malicious or biased data in training sets corrupting model behavior

owasp.name: Model Denial of Service
owasp.desc: Resource exhaustion via computationally expensive prompts or requests

owasp.name: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
owasp.desc: Compromised models, plugins, or dependencies from untrusted sources

owasp.name: Sensitive Information Disclosure
owasp.desc: LLM revealing confidential data from training data or active context

owasp.name: Insecure Plugin Design
owasp.desc: Plugins with excessive permissions or inadequate authorization controls

owasp.name: Excessive Agency
owasp.desc: Agents taking unintended high-impact actions without adequate human oversight

owasp.name: Overreliance
owasp.desc: Blind trust in LLM outputs without validation or fallback procedures

owasp.name: Model Theft
owasp.desc: Unauthorized extraction of model weights, parameters, or capabilities
